enrichment.short_description: A free, open-source, self-hosted redirect management service for creating, filtering, and controlling domain-level HTTP redirects from a lightweight Docker dashboard.
enrichment.seo_title: re:Director Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Self-Hosted Redirect Management
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what re:Director offers for self-hosted redirect management, including features, Docker deployment, licensing and how it compares for teams managing domain redirects.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: re:Director is a free, open-source, self-hosted redirect management service designed for developers, marketers and site operators who need centralized control over domain-level HTTP redirects. Released under the Apache License 2.0, the project is maintained by developer Jens Knipper (GitHub handle JensKnipper) and hosted at github.com/re-Director/re-director, with documentation published at re-director.github.io. As of mid-2026 the repository has accumulated roughly 45 GitHub stars and 5 forks across 9 tagged releases, with the latest version, v0.0.10, published on June 8, 2026. The core promise of re:Director is simplicity: connect any domain, define where it should redirect to, and manage that redirect through a lightweight web dashboard, without needing a separate database or complex infrastructure. The backend is written in Java using the Spring Boot framework and built with Maven, requiring Java 25 to run directly, though most users deploy it as a Docker container pulled from Docker Hub under the jensknipper/re-director image, optionally orchestrated with Docker Compose for persistent storage. Inside the dashboard, users can create redirects tied to specific source and target domains, filter the redirect list by source, target or active status, and pause or resume individual redirects without deleting their configuration. re:Director supports all four common HTTP redirect status codes, 301 permanent, 302 temporary, 307 temporary method-preserving, and 308 permanent method-preserving, giving operators fine-grained control over how search engines and browsers treat each redirect. The interface is mobile-responsive and includes an automatic dark mode that follows system preferences. Because it has no formal pricing plans, company structure or sales team, re:Director is best understood as a developer tool rather than a commercial SaaS product: there is no free trial to speak of because the entire tool is free, and there is no enterprise tier because support is community-driven through GitHub issues and the project documentation. It targets the same audience as commercial link-redirect and domain-forwarding services, such as marketing teams consolidating expired campaign domains, agencies managing client rebrands, or engineering teams wanting an internal, self-hosted alternative to third-party redirect SaaS tools, but positions itself specifically around self-hosting, data ownership and avoiding vendor lock-in for something as fundamental as domain redirects.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: re:Director is a self-hosted, Dockerized redirect service that lets anyone connect a domain and define where traffic to it should go, without a database, license fee or vendor account. It is fully open source under the Apache License 2.0 and maintained by a single developer, Jens Knipper, with source code on GitHub and documentation at re-director.github.io.

The project is aimed at technically comfortable users, such as developers, sysadmins and marketing-ops teams, who want a lightweight internal alternative to commercial link-redirect SaaS tools while keeping full ownership of redirect data and infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core functionality centers on redirect creation and lifecycle management: users define a source domain and target URL, choose an HTTP status code (301, 302, 307 or 308), and the redirect is live immediately. Redirects can be filtered by source, target or status, and paused or resumed without deleting the configuration.

The tool ships as a Docker image (jensknipper/re-director) built on a Java/Spring Boot backend, requires no external database, and includes a responsive, dark-mode-aware web dashboard that works on both desktop and mobile.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: re:Director has no pricing tiers because the entire tool is free and open source. Users self-host it on their own infrastructure (a VPS, home server or internal cluster), so the only ongoing cost is hosting and maintenance, not a license fee to the project itself.

features.title: Custom domain redirects
features.description: Connect any domain and define exactly where it should redirect to, all managed from a central dashboard.

features.title: Selectable HTTP status codes
features.description: Choose between 301, 302, 307 or 308 responses so search engines and browsers treat each redirect correctly.

features.title: Filtering by source, target or status
features.description: Quickly locate a specific redirect in large lists by filtering on source domain, destination or active/paused state.

features.title: Pause and resume redirects
features.description: Temporarily disable a redirect without losing its configuration, then re-enable it later with one click.

features.title: No external database required
features.description: Runs as a single Docker container with no separate database dependency, simplifying deployment and backups.

features.title: Dark mode and responsive UI
features.description: The dashboard automatically follows system light/dark preferences and works on mobile as well as desktop.

features.title: Docker-first deployment
features.description: Ships as an official Docker Hub image with Docker Compose examples for persistent, reproducible deployments.

features.title: Apache 2.0 licensing
features.description: Fully open-source code that can be inspected, modified and redistributed without licensing fees.

pros: Completely free with no pricing tiers or paywalls
Simple, single-container Docker deployment with no database to manage
Supports all major redirect status codes (301, 302, 307, 308)
Open-source under Apache 2.0, so the code can be audited or modified
Lightweight, focused feature set that avoids unnecessary complexity
cons: Maintained by a single developer, which is a bustus-factor risk for production reliance
No official hosted/managed version, so self-hosting knowledge is required
Small community (under 50 GitHub stars) means fewer third-party guides and integrations
No built-in analytics or click-tracking for redirects
No official SLA, support contract or enterprise tier for businesses that need guarantees

faqs.question: Is re:Director free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. re:Director is fully free and open source under the Apache License 2.0, with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: Do I need a database to run re:Director?
faqs.answer: No. It is designed to run without an external database, simplifying deployment to a single Docker container.

faqs.question: Which HTTP redirect codes does re:Director support?
faqs.answer: It supports 301, 302, 307 and 308 redirects, covering both permanent and temporary redirect scenarios.

faqs.question: Can I pause a redirect without deleting it?
faqs.answer: Yes, redirects can be paused and resumed at any time while keeping their configuration intact.

faqs.question: Is there a hosted version of re:Director?
faqs.answer: No official hosted version exists; the project is designed to be self-hosted via Docker on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: Who maintains re:Director?
faqs.answer: It is maintained by developer Jens Knipper and published on GitHub under the re-Director organization.

faqs.question: What technology is re:Director built with?
faqs.answer: The backend is written in Java using Spring Boot and built with Maven; it is distributed as a Docker image.

faqs.question: Does re:Director support mobile devices?
faqs.answer: Yes, the dashboard is responsive and usable on mobile browsers, and it also includes an automatic dark mode.

use_cases.description: Run a lightweight internal redirect layer for microsites, short links or legacy URLs without adding a new vendor dependency.

enrichment.seo_title: Reactive Resume Review 2026: Free Open-Source Resume Builder Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Reactive Resume's features, templates, self-hosting options and pricing for this free, open-source, privacy-first resume builder at rxresu.me.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Reactive Resume is a free, open-source resume builder created by developer Amruth Pillai, with the project's public development traceable back to 2019 and its first major public release, version 2, launched in mid-2020. The tool is hosted at rxresu.me and its source code lives at github.com/AmruthPillai/Reactive-Resume, where it has grown into one of the most popular projects in its category with roughly 39.7 thousand GitHub stars and 4.5 thousand forks. It is distributed under the MIT license, meaning anyone can inspect, modify, self-host or redistribute the code freely. The product itself lets users pick from more than fifteen professionally designed templates, playfully named after Pokemon such as Azurill, Bronzor, Chikorita, Ditto, Gengar, Kakuna, Lapras, Onyx and Pikachu, fill in resume sections through a real-time editor with live preview, reorder sections via drag-and-drop, and export the finished document as a PDF, JSON or DOCX file. Basic use requires no account creation at all: a user can build and export a resume anonymously. For people who want to save and sync resumes across sessions, Reactive Resume supports account-based storage plus AI-assisted content suggestions through configurable integrations with OpenAI, Google Gemini and Anthropic Claude. The current architecture is built on TanStack Start (React 19 with Vite), Node.js and TypeScript on the backend, PostgreSQL with Drizzle ORM for data storage, ORPC for type-safe client-server communication, Better Auth for authentication (including passkey support), and Tailwind CSS with a Base UI component system for styling; state is managed with Zustand and TanStack Query. Anyone concerned about data privacy can self-host the entire stack via Docker, with PostgreSQL as the database and an optional SeaweedFS instance for S3-compatible file storage; as of version 5.1.0, PDF generation happens client-side in the browser rather than through an external print/rendering service, further reducing what data ever leaves the user's device. The interface supports multiple languages through community-driven translation on Crowdin, and the app includes a dark mode. Because there are no premium tiers, subscription fees or paywalled templates, Reactive Resume is positioned squarely as a free alternative to commercial resume builders like Novoresume, Zety or Canva's resume tools, appealing most to job seekers, students and privacy-conscious users who want full control over their resume data and are comfortable with, or actively prefer, an open-source, self-hostable tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Reactive Resume is an open-source resume builder created by developer Amruth Pillai, with public development dating back to 2019 and a major version 2 relaunch in 2020. It is released under the MIT license and has grown to roughly 39.7 thousand GitHub stars, making it one of the most widely used open-source resume tools available.

The product is designed around privacy and portability: basic resume creation and export require no account, and the entire application can be self-hosted via Docker for users who want to keep their data fully under their own control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users choose from 15-plus templates, edit content in a real-time editor with live preview and drag-and-drop section reordering, and export to PDF, JSON or DOCX. Optional AI assistance can be configured through OpenAI, Google Gemini or Anthropic Claude API keys to help refine resume content.

The stack is built on TanStack Start (React 19/Vite), PostgreSQL with Drizzle ORM, and Better Auth with passkey support; self-hosters run it via Docker with an optional SeaweedFS instance for S3-compatible storage, and translations are crowd-sourced via Crowdin.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Reactive Resume is completely free, whether used on the hosted rxresu.me instance or self-hosted on private infrastructure. There are no premium templates, subscription tiers or paywalls; the project is sustained by its open-source community rather than a commercial pricing model.

features.description: The UI is localized into multiple languages through community contributions on Crowdin.

pros: Completely free with no premium tiers or paywalled templates
Open-source (MIT license) with a large, active GitHub community
No account required for basic resume creation and export
Fully self-hostable for users who want complete data ownership
Wide template selection and multiple export formats (PDF, JSON, DOCX)
cons: No official paid support plan or SLA for businesses or teams
Self-hosting requires Docker and PostgreSQL knowledge for full control
AI features depend on users supplying their own third-party API keys
Not built for team or agency-scale resume/candidate management
Community support only, since there is no dedicated commercial vendor

faqs.question: Is Reactive Resume really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is completely free and open source under the MIT license, whether used on rxresu.me or self-hosted.

faqs.question: Do I need to create an account to use Reactive Resume?
faqs.answer: No, basic resume building and PDF export can be done without an account, preserving user privacy.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Reactive Resume?
faqs.answer: Yes, the full application can be self-hosted via Docker with a PostgreSQL database and optional S3-compatible storage.

faqs.question: What export formats does Reactive Resume support?
faqs.answer: Resumes can be exported as PDF, JSON or DOCX, and shared via a public link.

faqs.question: Does Reactive Resume use AI?
faqs.answer: It can optionally integrate with OpenAI, Google Gemini or Anthropic Claude using your own API key for content assistance.

faqs.question: Who created Reactive Resume?
faqs.answer: It was created by developer Amruth Pillai, with public development beginning in 2019.

faqs.question: What technology stack powers Reactive Resume?
faqs.answer: It runs on TanStack Start (React 19/Vite), Node.js, TypeScript, PostgreSQL with Drizzle ORM, and Better Auth.

faqs.question: Is Reactive Resume available in multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, the interface is translated into multiple languages through community contributions on Crowdin.

enrichment.short_description: A lightweight, self-hosted, open-source read-it-later and bookmark manager that saves clean, ad-free copies of articles, images and videos.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source (self-hosted); paid hosted tier planned
enrichment.seo_title: Readeck Review 2026: Self-Hosted Open-Source Read-It-Later App
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what Readeck offers as a self-hosted, open-source alternative to Pocket, including features, licensing, mobile apps and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Readeck is a self-hosted, open-source bookmark and read-it-later application that lets users save the readable content of web pages, images and videos so they can revisit them later in a clean, standardized, stripped-down layout free of ads and trackers. The project's primary source repository lives on Codeberg at codeberg.org/readeck/readeck rather than GitHub, reflecting its independent, community-driven development model, though a GitHub mirror exists and has attracted roughly 265 stars. Readeck is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3), which requires that any modified, network-deployed version of the code also make its source available, a common choice for self-hosted web applications that want to prevent proprietary forks. The core workflow mirrors better-known read-it-later services like Pocket or Wallabag: users save a link, typically via a browser extension or bookmarklet, and Readeck fetches and stores a clean, readable copy of that page's content, organizing it with tags, favorites and an archive state. Readeck also supports highlighting passages within saved articles so key excerpts can be found again quickly, following saved content via RSS and OPDS feeds for e-reader compatibility, and exporting saved articles to e-book formats for offline reading. The application ships as a single Docker container with no additional database service required, and reviewers note it can be set up in under an hour, with save-to-library actions completing in just a few seconds once configured. Companion mobile clients extend the ecosystem: an Android app is distributed on F-Droid and other Android app stores, built by a third-party developer as a client for the self-hosted Readeck service, and a native iOS app is available on the Apple App Store for iPhone and iPad users who want professional-grade bookmark management on mobile. As of mid-2026, Readeck remains a self-hosted-only product; there is no first-party hosted SaaS version, meaning every user runs their own instance, typically via Docker on a home server, NAS or VPS. According to the project's own roadmap, the team behind Readeck plans to introduce a paid, company-hosted tier sometime in 2026 for users who want a managed instance without operating their own server, which would represent Readeck's first commercial offering alongside its continued free, self-hosted, open-source core. The tool has been highlighted by several self-hosting and productivity outlets as one of the more polished, lightweight alternatives in the read-it-later category, particularly for users who have grown wary of the pricing changes or shutdowns affecting commercial bookmarking services.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Readeck is an open-source, self-hosted read-it-later and bookmark manager that saves clean, readable copies of web articles, images and videos so users can revisit them without ads, trackers or the risk of link rot. Development happens primarily on Codeberg rather than GitHub, and the project is licensed under AGPLv3.

It positions itself as a lightweight, easy-to-run alternative to services like Pocket or Wallabag, deployable as a single Docker container with no separate database dependency, and is aimed at privacy-conscious users comfortable running their own server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Saved content can be organized with tags, marked as favorites, or moved to an archive, and users can highlight important passages for later reference. Readeck exposes saved content via RSS and OPDS feeds and supports exporting articles to e-book formats for offline reading on e-readers.

Native and third-party clients extend the core web app: a native iOS app is available on the App Store, and an Android client is distributed through F-Droid, giving users mobile access to their self-hosted library alongside the browser extension used to save links.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Readeck itself is free and open source; the only cost is the infrastructure needed to self-host it, such as a home server, NAS or small VPS. The project has announced plans to launch a paid, company-hosted tier in 2026 for users who prefer a managed instance, but as of now there is no official hosted SaaS product.

features.description: Highlight key passages within saved articles so important excerpts can be found again quickly.

features.title: RSS and OPDS feed support
features.description: Follow your saved-article library through RSS, or connect it to e-readers via OPDS.

features.title: E-book export
features.description: Export saved articles to e-book formats for offline reading on dedicated e-reader devices.

features.title: Single-container Docker deployment
features.description: Runs as one Docker container with no separate database service required, simplifying self-hosting.

features.title: Native mobile apps
features.description: A native iOS app and a community-built Android client provide mobile access to your Readeck library.

features.title: REST API
features.description: A documented API lets developers integrate Readeck into their own tools and automation workflows.

pros: Free and open source under the AGPLv3 license
Very easy to self-host, typically running as a single Docker container
Clean, ad-free and tracker-free reading experience
Native iOS app and a community Android client for mobile access
Supports highlights, tags, RSS and OPDS export for e-readers
cons: No first-party hosted plan yet, so self-hosting is currently mandatory
Requires basic server administration skills such as Docker and reverse proxying
Smaller community and ecosystem compared to established tools like Pocket or Wallabag
Mobile apps are relatively new and less mature than the core web app
Upcoming paid hosted tier pricing has not yet been announced

faqs.question: Is Readeck free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Readeck is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license; the only cost is hosting it yourself.

faqs.question: Does Readeck offer a hosted version?
faqs.answer: Not yet. As of mid-2026 Readeck is self-hosted only, though the project has announced plans for a paid, company-hosted tier in 2026.

faqs.question: How do I install Readeck?
faqs.answer: Readeck runs as a single Docker container with no separate database required, and can typically be set up in under an hour.

faqs.question: Does Readeck have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, there is a native iOS app on the App Store and a community-built Android client distributed via F-Droid.

faqs.question: Can I export my saved articles?
faqs.answer: Yes, Readeck supports exporting saved content to e-book formats and exposes libraries via RSS and OPDS feeds.

faqs.question: What license does Readeck use?
faqs.answer: Readeck is distributed under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3).

faqs.question: Where is Readeck's source code hosted?
faqs.answer: Primarily on Codeberg at codeberg.org/readeck/readeck, with a mirror also available on GitHub.

faqs.question: How is Readeck different from Pocket or Wallabag?
faqs.answer: Readeck focuses on being lightweight and easy to self-host, with a single-container Docker setup, native mobile apps and highlight support built in.

enrichment.short_description: An open-source, self-hosted news-reading and read-it-later platform built on Go and React, with webhook integrations and offline export.
enrichment.seo_title: Readflow Review 2026: Open-Source Self-Hosted Read-It-Later Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Readflow's features, integrations, licensing and self-hosting requirements for this open-source Go and React news-reading platform.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Readflow is an open-source, self-hosted news-reading and read-it-later platform created by developer Nicolas Carlier, with its GitHub repository at github.com/ncarlier/readflow first created in April 2019. The project is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3), with accompanying artwork released under Creative Commons terms. As of its most recent tagged release, v1.2.0 from March 4, 2024, the repository has accumulated roughly 474 GitHub stars, 34 forks and 682 commits, reflecting steady, focused development over several years rather than rapid commercial growth. Architecturally, Readflow's backend is written in Go, making up the majority of its codebase, paired with a TypeScript and React frontend and a GraphQL API for client-server communication; a PostgreSQL database is required for persistence. Self-hosters can deploy it several ways: compiling and running the Go binary directly, downloading pre-compiled binaries, or running it as a Docker container or via Docker Compose for a more turnkey setup. Functionally, Readflow centralizes article reading from multiple sources into one inbox-like interface, letting users save, organize and read content offline. Its standout feature is a flexible webhook system: incoming webhooks let external services push content into a user's Readflow queue (supporting sources like RSS feeds, Pocket, Wallabag and Shaarli), while outgoing webhooks let Readflow push saved or read articles out to other destinations, including S3-compatible storage buckets, enabling custom automation pipelines. Saved articles can be exported for offline reading in multiple formats, including HTML, PDF, EPUB and ZIP archives, and the application supports push notifications when new articles arrive. On the client side, Readflow is delivered as a Progressive Web App, giving it an app-like experience on both desktop and mobile without requiring a native app store listing, and it deliberately avoids ads and trackers to keep the reading experience clean. For power users and automation-minded self-hosters, the project also ships a companion CLI tool called readflow-ctl for scripting and bulk operations, plus a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that allows AI assistants to interact with a user's Readflow instance programmatically. Because Readflow has no commercial entity behind it, hosted plan, or pricing tiers, it is entirely free to run, with the only cost being the infrastructure needed to host the Go backend, React frontend and PostgreSQL database, most commonly on a home server, NAS or small VPS alongside other self-hosted services in a typical self-hosting stack such as those cataloged on awesome-selfhosted lists.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Readflow is an open-source, self-hosted news-reading and read-it-later platform created by developer Nicolas Carlier, with development on GitHub dating back to April 2019. It is licensed under AGPLv3 and built on a Go backend with a React/TypeScript frontend and a GraphQL API.

The tool centralizes article reading from multiple sources into a single interface and is aimed at self-hosters who want a customizable, webhook-driven alternative to commercial read-it-later services like Pocket.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Readflow's incoming and outgoing webhook system connects it to RSS feeds, Pocket, Wallabag, Shaarli and S3-compatible storage, enabling flexible automation pipelines for how content enters and leaves the platform. Saved articles can be exported offline as HTML, PDF, EPUB or ZIP files.

The app is delivered as a Progressive Web App for a consistent mobile and desktop experience, supports push notifications for new articles, and includes a companion CLI (readflow-ctl) plus an MCP server for AI assistant integration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Readflow is completely free and open source; there is no company or hosted SaaS offering behind it. Users self-host the Go binary, Docker container, or Docker Compose stack on their own infrastructure, requiring a PostgreSQL database.

No official hosted SaaS version or vendor support
Smaller community (under 500 GitHub stars) than mainstream alternatives
No native mobile apps, relying instead on the PWA experience

faqs.question: Is Readflow free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Readflow is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license, with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: Who created Readflow?
faqs.answer: Readflow is created and maintained by developer Nicolas Carlier, with development dating back to 2019.

faqs.question: What database does Readflow require?
faqs.answer: Readflow requires a PostgreSQL database for persistence alongside its Go backend.

faqs.question: Can Readflow connect to other read-it-later services?
faqs.answer: Yes, its webhook system supports integrations with RSS, Pocket, Wallabag, Shaarli and S3-compatible storage.

faqs.question: Does Readflow have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: There is no native mobile app; instead, Readflow is delivered as a Progressive Web App for mobile and desktop use.

faqs.question: What export formats does Readflow support?
faqs.answer: Saved articles can be exported offline as HTML, PDF, EPUB or ZIP files.

faqs.question: How do I self-host Readflow?
faqs.answer: You can run the Go binary directly, use a Docker container, or deploy via Docker Compose.

faqs.question: Does Readflow support automation or AI tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, it ships with a readflow-ctl CLI and an MCP server that lets AI assistants interact with your instance.

enrichment.short_description: An interactive API documentation platform that turns static developer docs into live, testable API reference hubs used by thousands of API-first companies.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Starter plan); paid plans from $250/month billed annually
enrichment.seo_title: ReadMe Review 2026: Interactive API Documentation Platform Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how ReadMe's interactive API documentation platform works, including pricing plans, key features, and how it compares for developer-facing docs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ReadMe is an interactive API documentation platform founded by Gregory Koberger, with the company's origins dating to 2014 and its formal debut through Y Combinator's Winter 2015 batch in San Francisco, California, where it remains headquartered. The company has raised roughly $10 million in total funding, including a $1.2 million seed round in 2014 and a $9 million Series A led by Accel announced in August 2019, and today employs somewhere between 70 and 83 people according to various company-data aggregators, distributed across multiple continents in a largely remote-friendly setup. ReadMe's core product converts static developer documentation into interactive, customizable reference hubs where developers can execute live API calls, view real responses, and understand integration requirements directly inside the docs rather than switching to a separate API client. The platform is used by more than 4,000 companies, including large, well-known technology brands such as Microsoft and Akamai, spanning everything from early-stage startups to Fortune 500 enterprises that need to make their APIs approachable to third-party developers. Beyond interactive reference docs, ReadMe includes usage analytics that show which endpoints developers actually test, where they get stuck, and which documentation sections drive the most engagement, giving API product teams data to prioritize documentation improvements. As of its current pricing structure published directly on readme.com/pricing, ReadMe offers three tiers: a free Starter plan that includes a custom domain, bi-directional sync, the interactive API reference, usage metrics, a markdown editor, customizable themes and baseline AI features such as an AI dropdown, LLMs.txt support and an MCP server; a Pro plan priced at $250 per month on annual billing that adds team member invitations, branching and review workflows, notifications, private documentation, a landing page, changelog and forum modules, custom MDX components, reusable content blocks, custom CSS/HTML support, and expanded AI capabilities like Ask AI Lite, an AI writing agent called Owlbert, an AI linter, and a GitHub-based AI writer; and an Enterprise plan starting at $3,000 or more per month on annual contracts that adds multi-project management, removal of ReadMe branding, granular user roles and access controls, branch review permissions, a global reusable content library, audit logs, SSO and OAuth support, dedicated support and implementation services, plus enterprise-grade AI tooling such as documentation audits, private AI context and global lint rules. An optional Ask AI add-on is available across tiers for an additional $150 per month. This tiered structure positions ReadMe as a premium, feature-rich alternative to simpler documentation generators, aimed specifically at companies whose product is an API and whose developer experience directly affects adoption and revenue.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ReadMe is an interactive API documentation platform founded by Gregory Koberger, with company origins in 2014 and a public launch through Y Combinator's Winter 2015 batch. Headquartered in San Francisco, California, the company has raised roughly $10 million in funding, including a 2019 Series A led by Accel, and employs an estimated 70 to 83 people.
The platform is used by more than 4,000 companies, ranging from startups to large enterprises such as Microsoft and Akamai, to turn static API docs into interactive, testable reference hubs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ReadMe's core differentiator is letting developers execute live API calls and see real responses directly inside the documentation, rather than switching to a separate API client or Postman collection. Built-in analytics show which endpoints get tested, where developers get stuck, and which sections drive the most engagement.

Higher tiers add collaboration features like branching and review workflows, private documentation, custom MDX and CSS support, changelog and forum modules, and a growing suite of AI tools including an AI writing agent, AI linter and documentation audits.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ReadMe offers a free Starter plan with the core interactive reference and basic AI features, a Pro plan at $250 per month billed annually that unlocks team collaboration and expanded AI tooling, and an Enterprise plan starting at $3,000 or more per month on annual contracts with SSO, audit logs and dedicated support.
An Ask AI add-on is available across all tiers for an additional $150 per month for teams that want the platform's fuller AI-assistant capabilities without moving to a higher plan.

features.description: Developers can execute live API calls and see real responses directly inside the documentation.

features.title: Usage analytics
features.description: Tracks which endpoints are tested, where developers get stuck, and which docs sections drive engagement.

features.title: Branching and review workflows
features.description: Teams can branch documentation changes and route them through review before publishing, similar to a code review flow.

features.title: Custom MDX and CSS support
features.description: Extend documentation pages with custom MDX components and CSS/HTML for fully branded experiences.

features.title: AI writing and linting tools
features.description: Includes an AI writing agent (Owlbert), AI linter, and GitHub-based AI writer to help produce and maintain docs.

features.title: Changelog, forum and landing page modules
features.description: Built-in modules for publishing changelogs, hosting developer forums and building a docs landing page.

features.title: SSO, audit logs and access controls
features.description: Enterprise-grade security features including SSO/OAuth, audit logs and granular user roles.

features.title: Multi-project management
features.description: Enterprise customers can manage documentation for multiple products or APIs from one account.

pros: Live, interactive API testing embedded directly in the documentation
Detailed analytics on developer engagement with the docs
Trusted by well-known enterprises such as Microsoft and Akamai
Free Starter tier available for smaller projects
Extensive AI tooling for writing, linting and auditing docs
cons: Paid plans are billed annually, which can be a barrier for smaller teams
Pro and Enterprise pricing is significantly higher than many documentation competitors
Enterprise pricing starts at $3,000+/month and requires a custom contract
Ask AI is a separate paid add-on rather than included by default on lower tiers
Full branding removal requires an upgrade from the free Starter plan

faqs.question: How much does ReadMe cost?
faqs.answer: ReadMe offers a free Starter plan, a Pro plan at $250/month billed annually, and Enterprise pricing starting at $3,000+/month on annual contracts.

faqs.question: When was ReadMe founded?
faqs.answer: ReadMe traces its origins to 2014 and launched publicly through Y Combinator's Winter 2015 batch, founded by Gregory Koberger.

faqs.question: Where is ReadMe headquartered?
faqs.answer: ReadMe is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Does ReadMe offer a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Starter plan is free and includes the interactive API reference, usage metrics and basic AI features.

faqs.question: What is Ask AI on ReadMe?
faqs.answer: Ask AI is an AI-assistant add-on available across ReadMe plans for an additional $150/month, with a lighter version included on Pro.

faqs.question: Which companies use ReadMe?
faqs.answer: ReadMe is used by more than 4,000 companies, including large enterprises like Microsoft and Akamai.

faqs.question: Does ReadMe support single sign-on?
faqs.answer: SSO and OAuth support are available on the Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: Can I remove ReadMe branding from my docs?
faqs.answer: Branding removal is available starting on the Enterprise plan, not on the free Starter tier.

enrichment.short_description: A no-code, browser-based design tool for building animated websites, portfolios, presentations and digital publications without writing code.
enrichment.seo_title: Readymag Review 2026: No-Code Website and Design Tool Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Readymag's no-code design tool works for websites, portfolios and presentations, including pricing plans, animation features and company background.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Readymag is a browser-based, no-code design platform for building websites, digital portfolios, presentations and online publications, founded in 2012 by Ukrainian designer Anton Herasymenko alongside co-founders Diana Kasay and Kirill Danchenko, and launched publicly in 2013. Originally conceived as a tool for creating digital magazines, hence the name, it evolved over the following decade into a broader web design tool competing with the likes of Webflow and Squarespace but leaning heavily into freeform, animation-rich page design rather than rigid templates. The company is headquartered in New York City at 155 Water Street and employs roughly 50 people. Notably, Readymag started as a bootstrapped company and, as of 2024, has bought out all of its external investors, making it fully founder-owned and self-funded rather than venture-backed, a detail the company emphasizes as evidence of sustainable, profitable growth rather than reliance on outside capital. Readymag's defining strength is its animation and interaction toolkit: designers can build scroll-triggered effects, page transitions, overlays and complex multi-step interactions entirely through a visual interface, without touching code, while still being able to export code or embed content via iFrame on higher-tier plans for teams that do want code-level control. The platform ships with a built-in font library that includes Google Fonts, Adobe Fonts and a range of independent type foundries, plus the ability to upload custom fonts, giving designers substantial typographic flexibility. On the commercial side, Readymag offers a free plan with no credit card required for people who want to experiment with the tool before connecting a custom domain, and paid plans that unlock domain mapping, additional collaborators and higher publishing limits. As of the most recent published pricing, the Personal plan costs $14 per month and supports one custom domain on one site with SSL and basic e-commerce for up to 10 products; the Freelancer plan costs $22 per month and is aimed at people building sites for clients, supporting five published websites, three mapped custom domains, two collaborators and 25,000 project views per month; and the Advanced (Business) plan costs $58.50 per month for larger teams, offering unlimited published websites, ten custom domains, ten collaborators, code export, iFrame embedding and priority support. An Enterprise plan with custom pricing is also available for the largest organizations, and all subscription tiers offer roughly a 10 percent discount when billed annually instead of monthly. This combination of no-code animation tooling, flexible typography and a founder-owned, profitable business model positions Readymag as a design-forward alternative for studios, freelancers and brands that want visually distinctive websites without hiring a developer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Readymag is a browser-based, no-code design platform founded in 2012 by Anton Herasymenko, Diana Kasay and Kirill Danchenko, and launched publicly in 2013 as a tool for digital magazines before broadening into a general-purpose website and presentation design tool. The company is headquartered in New York City and employs roughly 50 people.

As of 2024, Readymag has bought out all external investors and operates as a fully founder-owned, bootstrapped company, positioning it as a design-forward, no-code alternative to platforms like Webflow or Squarespace with a particular emphasis on animation and freeform layout.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Readymag is best known for its animation and interaction tools, letting designers build scroll effects, transitions, overlays and multi-step interactions visually, without code. Higher-tier plans also support code export and iFrame embedding for teams that want more technical control.

The platform includes a built-in font library spanning Google Fonts, Adobe Fonts and independent type foundries, plus custom font uploads, giving designers significant typographic flexibility for building distinctive, on-brand pages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Readymag offers a free plan with no credit card required, plus three main paid tiers: Personal at $14/month, Freelancer at $22/month and Advanced (Business) at $58.50/month, each unlocking more custom domains, collaborators and publishing limits. An Enterprise plan with custom pricing is also available.
All paid subscriptions can be billed monthly or annually, with annual billing offering roughly a 10 percent discount compared to paying month to month.

features.title: No-code animation and interactions
features.description: Build scroll effects, transitions, overlays and multi-step interactions visually without writing code.

features.description: Access Google Fonts, Adobe Fonts and independent type foundries, plus support for custom font uploads.

features.description: Connect one or more custom domains to published sites depending on plan tier.

features.title: Basic e-commerce
features.description: Sell up to a limited number of products directly from a Readymag site on entry-level plans.

features.title: Code export and iFrame embedding
features.description: Higher-tier plans allow exporting code or embedding published pages via iFrame for more technical workflows.

features.title: Multi-collaborator support
features.description: Invite collaborators to work on projects together, with collaborator limits increasing on higher tiers.

features.title: Public profile publishing
features.description: Publish multiple sites under one shared profile domain without mapping a custom domain to each.

features.title: Priority support on top tiers
features.description: The Advanced/Business plan includes priority customer support for larger teams and agencies.

pros: Strong no-code animation and interaction tooling not commonly found in competitors
Large built-in font library including Google Fonts and Adobe Fonts
Free plan available with no credit card required
Founder-owned and bootstrapped, suggesting stable, profitable operations
Flexible for both simple portfolios and complex, animation-heavy sites
cons: Paid plans are needed for custom domains, limiting the free tier's practical use
Freelancer and Business plans still cap collaborators and published-site domain mapping
Less suited to complex e-commerce compared to dedicated platforms like Shopify
Steeper learning curve for advanced animation features compared to simple template builders
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires direct contact

faqs.question: How much does Readymag cost?
faqs.answer: Readymag has a free plan, then paid tiers starting at $14/month (Personal), $22/month (Freelancer) and $58.50/month (Advanced/Business), with an Enterprise plan available on request.

faqs.question: When was Readymag founded?
faqs.answer: Readymag was founded in 2012 and launched publicly in 2013 by Anton Herasymenko, Diana Kasay and Kirill Danchenko.

faqs.question: Where is Readymag headquartered?
faqs.answer: Readymag is headquartered in New York City, at 155 Water Street.

faqs.question: Is Readymag a coding tool?
faqs.answer: No, Readymag is a no-code, browser-based design tool, though higher-tier plans allow exporting code for developers who want it.

faqs.question: Does Readymag support e-commerce?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Personal plan supports basic e-commerce for up to 10 products, with higher limits on upper tiers.

faqs.question: Is Readymag venture-backed?
faqs.answer: No, as of 2024 Readymag has bought out all external investors and is fully founder-owned and bootstrapped.

faqs.question: Can I use my own domain with Readymag?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid plans support mapping one or more custom domains depending on the tier.

faqs.question: Does Readymag offer a discount for annual billing?
faqs.answer: Yes, annual billing offers roughly a 10 percent discount compared to monthly billing.

enrichment.short_description: A free, open-source, self-hosted Plex downloader that pulls media from any accessible Plex server into your own library, also working as a Sonarr/Radarr indexer and download client.
languages: English, French, German, Polish, with more in progress
enrichment.seo_title: Reaparr Review 2026: Open-Source Plex Downloader for Sonarr and Radarr
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what Reaparr does, how it integrates with Sonarr and Radarr, its features, licensing, self-hosting requirements and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Reaparr is a free, open-source, self-hosted application described on its own website, reaparr.rocks, as a Plex downloader that brings content from any accessible Plex server to a user's own library. Its GitHub organization is Reaparr, with the primary repository at github.com/Reaparr/Reaparr, distributed under the GPL-3.0 open-source license. Notably, the project was not originally called Reaparr: it operated under a different, Plex-referencing name until it received a trademark complaint from Plex Inc., prompting a rename. The team settled on Reaparr, a deliberate play on the word Reaper with the extra arr appended to match the naming convention used by the well-known *arr ecosystem of self-hosted media tools such as Sonarr and Radarr, both to sidestep the trademark issue and to signal the project's role within that same tooling family going forward. Functionally, Reaparr connects to one or more Plex accounts and servers a user has access to and lets them browse, filter and download media from those libraries into their own collection, using dual viewing modes, a poster grid and a table layout, with filtering and sorting by attributes like genre, year and quality, plus the ability to choose a distinct destination folder for each downloaded item. Downloads are handled by multiple parallel threads so a large queue does not stall on a single slow transfer, and the application automatically recovers from interrupted downloads rather than requiring a manual restart. Beyond acting as a standalone downloader, Reaparr can also function as an indexer and download client inside Sonarr and Radarr, meaning users can add items to their Sonarr or Radarr queue and have Reaparr pick them up and process the download automatically, effectively substituting for a traditional torrent or Usenet indexer within that workflow. The application is distributed as Docker images published under the Reaparr organization on Docker Hub, and it is explicitly designed to run cross-platform on Windows, Linux and macOS, as well as on NAS-oriented platforms like Unraid and Synology, and on both ARM and AMD-based hardware. The interface currently supports English, French, German and Polish, with additional languages planned. Reaparr has no pricing tiers or company behind it; its documentation site describes it as free and open source, sustained through voluntary donations from users who want to support continued development, hosting costs and documentation upkeep, with an active Discord community providing user support alongside GitHub issues and the project's own changelog and FAQ pages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Reaparr is a free, open-source, self-hosted Plex downloader that lets users pull media from any accessible Plex server into their own library. The project was previously distributed under a different, Plex-referencing name before a trademark complaint from Plex Inc. prompted a rename to Reaparr, chosen to echo the naming convention of the broader *arr media-tooling ecosystem such as Sonarr and Radarr.

The project is licensed under GPL-3.0, hosted on GitHub under the Reaparr organization, and distributed as Docker images for cross-platform deployment on Windows, Linux, macOS, Unraid and Synology systems across both ARM and AMD hardware.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Reaparr connects multiple Plex accounts and servers, letting users browse and download accessible media through a poster-grid or table view with filtering and sorting by genre, year and quality, and per-item destination folder selection. Downloads run across multiple parallel threads with automatic recovery from interruptions.

Beyond standalone downloading, Reaparr can act as an indexer and download client inside Sonarr and Radarr, letting those tools queue items for Reaparr to fetch automatically, functioning as an alternative to traditional torrent or Usenet indexers within that workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Reaparr is entirely free and open source, with no paid tiers or commercial entity behind it. The project is sustained through voluntary donations that fund ongoing development, hosting and documentation, and users self-host the application via Docker on their own hardware.

features.title: Cross-server Plex downloading
features.description: Connect multiple Plex accounts and servers and download accessible media into your own library.

features.title: Dual library views
features.description: Browse content in either a poster grid or a detailed table layout, whichever fits your workflow.

features.title: Advanced filtering and sorting
features.description: Filter and sort available media by genre, year, quality and other attributes before downloading.

features.title: Multi-threaded downloads with auto-recovery
features.description: Multiple parallel download threads keep the queue moving, with automatic recovery from interrupted transfers.

features.title: Sonarr and Radarr integration
features.description: Acts as an indexer and download client so Sonarr and Radarr can queue items for Reaparr to process automatically.

features.title: Per-item destination folders
features.description: Choose a specific destination folder for each downloaded item rather than a single fixed location.

features.title: Cross-platform Docker deployment
features.description: Runs via Docker on Windows, Linux, macOS, Unraid and Synology, across both ARM and AMD architectures.

features.title: Multi-language interface
features.description: Supports English, French, German and Polish, with additional languages planned.

pros: Completely free and open source under GPL-3.0
Integrates directly with Sonarr and Radarr as an indexer and download client
Multi-threaded downloads with automatic recovery from interruptions
Cross-platform support including NAS systems like Unraid and Synology
Active Discord community for user support
cons: Requires access to Plex servers with media you are permitted to download from
Self-hosting via Docker requires some technical setup knowledge
No official company, SLA or paid support tier
Smaller, newer project that previously had to rebrand due to a trademark dispute
Language support is currently limited to a handful of languages

faqs.question: Is Reaparr free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Reaparr is completely free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license, supported by voluntary donations.

faqs.question: Why is it called Reaparr instead of its original name?
faqs.answer: The project renamed itself to Reaparr after receiving a trademark complaint from Plex Inc., choosing a name that echoes the *arr naming convention of tools like Sonarr and Radarr.

faqs.question: Does Reaparr work with Sonarr and Radarr?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can function as an indexer and download client so Sonarr and Radarr can queue items for Reaparr to download automatically.

faqs.question: What platforms does Reaparr support?
faqs.answer: Reaparr runs via Docker on Windows, Linux, macOS, Unraid and Synology, supporting both ARM and AMD hardware.

faqs.question: What languages does Reaparr support?
faqs.answer: The interface currently supports English, French, German and Polish, with more languages planned.

faqs.question: Does Reaparr have a paid tier?
faqs.answer: No, Reaparr has no pricing tiers; it is entirely free and sustained through user donations.

faqs.question: How does Reaparr handle interrupted downloads?
faqs.answer: It automatically recovers from download interruptions rather than requiring users to manually restart the transfer.

faqs.question: Where can I get support for Reaparr?
faqs.answer: Support is available through an active Discord community, GitHub issues and the project's documentation and FAQ pages.

enrichment.short_description: An AI-powered applicant tracking system and CRM built specifically for recruitment and staffing agencies, used by firms across 80-plus countries.
enrichment.starting_price: $85/user/month (Pro plan, billed annually) or $100/user/month billed monthly
enrichment.headquarters: Norwood, New Jersey, USA and Pune, India (dual headquarters, fully remote team)
enrichment.seo_title: Recruit CRM Review 2026: Pricing, Features and AI Recruiting Software Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Recruit CRM's applicant tracking system and CRM works for staffing agencies, including pricing plans, AI features and company background.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Recruit CRM is an applicant tracking system and CRM built specifically for recruitment agencies and executive search firms, founded in 2017 and now used by recruiting businesses across more than 80 countries. The company maintains dual headquarters, with a US entity registered in Norwood, New Jersey, and an operational hub in Pune, India, while running as a fully remote SaaS company with employees distributed across multiple continents; recent company-data sources put headcount at roughly 365 to 372 employees as of early 2026, and the company has publicly stated it has been growing at around 200 percent year over year, a figure that reflects the company's own marketing claims rather than independently audited figures. Recruit CRM positions itself as an AI-first platform purpose-built for the staffing and executive search workflow, distinguishing it from generic CRM or ATS software aimed at internal corporate recruiting teams. Its feature set spans the full agency recruiting lifecycle: a Chrome sourcing extension for pulling candidate data directly from LinkedIn and other sites, an AI resume parser that extracts structured candidate data automatically, GPT-based integration for tasks like writing job descriptions or candidate outreach, deal pipelines for tracking client business alongside candidate pipelines, a fully integrated mailbox, built-in phone calling and call recording, a reporting suite, unlimited open jobs, and direct job board posting. Pricing is structured in three tiers billed per user. The entry-level Pro plan starts at $85 per user per month when billed annually, or $100 per user per month on monthly billing, and includes the Chrome sourcing extension, AI resume parsing, GPT integration, unlimited email templates, an integrated mailbox, deal pipelines, phone calling and recording, a complete reporting suite, unlimited open jobs, job board posting, one hiring pipeline, up to two candidate matches and up to 15 custom fields. The Business plan, listed as the company's most popular tier, starts at roughly $125 per user per month annually (around $150 monthly) and adds automated email sequencing, bulk texting, an executive search report generator, resume formatting tools, custom roles and teams, multiple hiring pipelines, IP whitelisting, audit logs, single sign-on, additional email connections, up to 50 candidate matches, up to 150 custom fields, removal of Recruit CRM branding, and open API access. The top-tier Enterprise plan, priced from roughly $165 per user per month and requiring a custom demo for exact pricing, adds workflow automation with 1,000 included tasks per user per month, advanced analytics, LinkedIn messaging integration, phone and email data enrichment with 500 credits per user per month, calling and texting credits, an unlimited-jobs Recruit Craft module, job multiposting with 10 jobs per user per month included, and a dedicated account manager. A free trial is available across plans before committing to a paid subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Recruit CRM is an applicant tracking system and CRM built specifically for recruitment agencies and executive search firms, founded in 2017. It maintains dual headquarters in Norwood, New Jersey and Pune, India, operating as a fully remote company with roughly 365 employees serving customers in more than 80 countries.

The platform is positioned as an AI-first recruiting tool purpose-built for agency workflows, combining candidate sourcing, an ATS, CRM-style deal pipelines and communication tools in one system, distinguishing it from generic corporate ATS software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include a Chrome sourcing extension, an AI resume parser, GPT-based integrations for content generation, deal pipelines alongside candidate pipelines, an integrated mailbox, and built-in phone calling with call recording. Higher tiers add automated email sequencing, bulk texting, executive search report generation and resume formatting.

Enterprise-level customers get workflow automation, advanced analytics, LinkedIn messaging integration, contact data enrichment credits, job multiposting and a dedicated account manager, reflecting the platform's focus on scaling larger staffing operations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Recruit CRM prices per user across three tiers: Pro starts at $85/user/month billed annually (or $100/user/month monthly), Business starts at roughly $125/user/month annually, and Enterprise starts at roughly $165/user/month with custom demo-based pricing.
All plans offer a discount for annual billing compared to monthly billing, and a free trial is available before subscribing to any paid tier.

features.title: Chrome sourcing extension
features.description: Pull candidate profiles and data directly from LinkedIn and other websites into Recruit CRM while browsing.

features.title: AI resume parser
features.description: Automatically extracts structured candidate data from uploaded resumes to speed up data entry.

features.title: GPT integration
features.description: Uses GPT-based AI to help generate job descriptions, candidate outreach messages and other recruiting content.

features.title: Deal and hiring pipelines
features.description: Track both client business deals and candidate hiring stages in configurable pipeline views.

features.title: Integrated mailbox and calling
features.description: Send and receive email plus make and record phone calls directly within the platform.

features.title: Automated email sequencing
features.description: Set up multi-step, automated email sequences to nurture candidates and clients over time (Business plan and up).

features.title: Executive search report generator
features.description: Produce client-ready executive search reports directly from candidate and search data (Business plan and up).

features.title: Workflow automation and data enrichment
features.description: Automate recurring tasks and enrich contact records with phone and email data on the Enterprise plan.

pros: Purpose-built specifically for recruitment and staffing agency workflows
Combines ATS, CRM deal pipelines and communication tools in one platform
AI resume parsing and GPT integration speed up candidate processing
Scales from small agencies (Pro) to large enterprise search firms
Free trial available before committing to a paid subscription
cons: Per-user pricing can get expensive quickly for larger agency teams
Entry-level Pro plan limits hiring pipelines and candidate matches
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a sales demo
Some advanced features like SSO and audit logs are gated behind the Business tier
Data enrichment and workflow automation credits are capped even on Enterprise

faqs.question: How much does Recruit CRM cost?
faqs.answer: Recruit CRM starts at $85/user/month billed annually (or $100/user/month monthly) for the Pro plan, with Business and Enterprise tiers priced higher per user.

faqs.question: When was Recruit CRM founded?
faqs.answer: Recruit CRM was founded in 2017.

faqs.question: Where is Recruit CRM headquartered?
faqs.answer: Recruit CRM maintains dual headquarters in Norwood, New Jersey, USA and Pune, India, with a fully remote team.

faqs.question: Who is Recruit CRM built for?
faqs.answer: It is built specifically for recruitment and staffing agencies and executive search firms, rather than generic internal corporate recruiting teams.

faqs.question: Does Recruit CRM include AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes an AI resume parser and GPT integration for generating recruiting content, with advanced analytics on higher tiers.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Recruit CRM?
faqs.answer: Yes, Recruit CRM offers a free trial before requiring a paid subscription.

faqs.question: What is included in the Business plan that Pro lacks?
faqs.answer: Business adds automated email sequencing, bulk texting, an executive search report generator, resume formatting, custom roles/teams, SSO and audit logs, among other features.

faqs.question: Does Recruit CRM integrate with LinkedIn?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a Chrome sourcing extension for pulling candidate data from LinkedIn, plus LinkedIn messaging integration on the Enterprise plan.

use_cases.title: Recruitment agency candidate pipeline management
use_cases.description: Track candidates through hiring pipelines from sourcing to placement, alongside client deal pipelines.

use_cases.title: Executive search reporting
use_cases.description: Generate client-ready executive search reports and format resumes for high-touch retained search engagements.

use_cases.title: Scaling multi-recruiter teams
use_cases.description: Coordinate custom roles, workflow automation and data enrichment across larger recruiting teams.
use_cases.audience: Growing staffing firms and enterprise recruiting teams

features: 1 hiring pipeline, up to 2 candidate matches
Up to 50 candidate matches, 150 custom fields
Branding removal and open API access
